name: World's Greatest Stretch
instructions: From a high plank position, step your right foot forward outside your right hand. Drop your left knee to the ground and keep your back leg long. Place your left hand on the ground for support and rotate your right arm up toward the ceiling, opening your chest and following your hand with your eyes. Hold the open position for a breath, then return the hand to the floor and drive your hips back to straighten the front leg, flexing the front toes toward you. Repeat for 5 reps per side, moving slowly and deliberately with each breath.
feelRight: You should feel a deep opening through the front hip flexor of the back leg and a stretch through the hamstring when you straighten the front leg. The thoracic rotation should feel smooth and unrestricted, as if your spine is unwinding one vertebra at a time.
feelWrong: If you rush through the movement or skip the hip drive-back, you lose the hamstring stretch entirely and shortchange the posterior chain. Keep the back leg active and don't let your front heel lift off the ground during the rotation.
notes: Think of this as a full-body reset — it hits hips, hamstrings, thoracic spine, and shoulders in one move. Breathe deeply into the end range of each position rather than forcing range.

name: Doorway Pec Openers
instructions: Stand in a doorway or next to a vertical post and place your right forearm against the frame at approximately shoulder height, elbow bent to 90 degrees. Keeping your torso tall and core braced, gently rotate your body away from the arm until you feel a stretch through the front of your chest and shoulder. Hold this position for 30 seconds while breathing deeply, then switch sides.
feelRight: You should feel a mild to moderate stretch across the pectoral muscles and front deltoid, with no pinching in the shoulder joint. The sensation should be broad and diffuse across the chest, not sharp or localized to the bicep tendon.
feelWrong: Common mistakes include shrugging the shoulder up toward the ear or leaning forward, which shifts the stretch to the neck and upper traps instead of the pecs. Keep the shoulder blade slightly retracted and down — imagine sliding it into your back pocket throughout the hold.
notes: If you feel a pinch in the front of the shoulder, raise or lower your arm angle slightly until it disappears. This is a staple for undoing desk posture — pair it with scapular retraction drills.

name: Cat-Cow Transitions
instructions: Start on all fours with your wrists directly under your shoulders and knees directly under your hips. Inhale as you drop your belly toward the floor, lift your tailbone and chest, and let your head come up gently (Cow). Exhale slowly as you round your spine toward the ceiling, tuck your chin to your chest, and tuck your tailbone under (Cat). Move one vertebra at a time, letting each motion be initiated by the breath rather than by muscular force.
feelRight: Each cycle should feel like a slow wave traveling from your tailbone to the crown of your head. You should feel length being created between each vertebra, and the motion should be entirely pain-free through the lower back.
feelWrong: Moving too fast or collapsing into the shoulders turns this into a mechanical rock instead of a spine articulation. Keep the weight even between both hands and both knees, and don't dump into one side — the movement should be symmetrical.
notes: Focus on initiating the movement from your pelvis and letting it ripple through. If you have wrist discomfort, make fists or use yoga blocks under your hands.

name: Thoracic Spine Rotation
instructions: Start on all fours with one hand behind your head (fingertips lightly touching the back of your skull). Keeping your hips completely square and stable, rotate your upper body by opening your elbow toward the ceiling, following the motion with your gaze. Pause briefly at the top of the rotation, then lower back down and repeat before switching sides.
feelRight: You should feel the rotation happening in your mid-back (thoracic spine) while your hips and lower back remain completely still. The movement should feel fluid and unrestricted, like a gentle twist through the ribcage.
feelWrong: If your hips shift or your lower back arches to compensate, you're rotating from the lumbar spine instead of the thoracic. Keep the non-working hand planted firmly and watch that your supporting arm doesn't collapse or hyperextend.
notes: This is essential for overhead pressing health and countering the effects of prolonged sitting. If you can't feel the rotation in your mid-back, try bringing your elbow higher or lowering the hand placement slightly.

name: Couch Stretch
instructions: Kneel facing away from a couch, bench, or sturdy box with your shins against it. Place the top of one foot on the surface behind you, then bring the other foot forward into a lunge position with your knee at 90 degrees. Keeping your torso upright and core engaged, gently drive your hips forward until you feel a deep stretch through the front of the hip and thigh of the back leg. Hold for 45 seconds, actively squeezing the glute of the stretching leg throughout to protect the lower back.
feelRight: The stretch should be deep but tolerable across the front of the hip, the quadriceps, and the psoas. Actively squeezing the glute on the stretching side should intensify the stretch at the front of the hip and signal the hip flexor to release.
feelWrong: Arching the lower back or letting the ribcage flare is the most common mistake — it shifts tension into the lumbar spine and defeats the purpose of the stretch. Keep your pelvis slightly tucked (posterior tilt) and your ribs zipped down throughout the hold.
notes: This is an intense stretch — scale it back by moving your front foot further away if the sensation is too sharp. If the surface is hard, pad the top of your foot with a folded towel.

name: Hip Circle Swings
instructions: Stand on one leg with a soft knee, holding onto a wall or sturdy surface for balance if needed. Lift the opposite leg slightly off the ground and begin making slow, controlled circles with your knee, drawing the largest arc you can manage without losing balance. Complete 10 circles in one direction, then 10 in the opposite direction before switching legs. Gradually increase the range of motion with each rep as the joint warms up.
feelRight: You should feel the hip joint moving smoothly through its full rotational range without any catching, clicking, or sharp pain. The sensation should be one of gentle opening and lubrication in the hip capsule, becoming smoother with each successive rep.
feelWrong: Compensating by tilting your torso or swinging your upper body to generate momentum means you're not actually moving through the hip. Keep your torso as still as possible and focus on moving only from the hip joint — smaller, controlled circles are better than big sloppy ones.
notes: This is a great dynamic warm-up before squats or deadlifts. If you have trouble balancing, keep the circle small and focus on control — quality over range.

name: Ankle Mobility Drills
instructions: Sit on the ground with one leg extended in front of you. Using your hand or by tracing with your big toe, draw 10 slow circles in each direction at the ankle joint, going through the full pain-free range of motion. Then perform 10 dorsiflexion rocks by placing your foot flat on the ground and driving your knee forward over your toes while keeping your heel planted, holding the end range for 2 seconds each rep.
feelRight: The circles should feel smooth and unrestricted through the talus joint with no grinding or catching. During the dorsiflexion rocks, you should feel a gentle stretch through the Achilles and calf at the end range, and your heel should remain firmly planted on the ground.
feelWrong: If your heel lifts off the ground during dorsiflexion, you're not getting true ankle range — back off slightly and focus on pushing the knee forward while keeping heel contact. Circling from the midfoot instead of the true ankle joint is common; keep the rest of your leg still and isolate the movement to the ankle.
reps: 10 circles each direction + 10 dorsiflexion rocks per side
notes: Ankle mobility is often the hidden limiter in squat depth. Do these daily — even a few minutes of consistent work yields noticeable improvements in squat mechanics and landing absorption.

name: Band Pull-Aparts
instructions: Stand tall with a light to moderate resistance band held in both hands at shoulder height, arms extended straight in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows. Keeping your arms straight and core braced, pull the band apart by driving your hands laterally toward the sides of the room, squeezing your shoulder blades together as you go. Pause briefly at full extension, then slowly return to the starting position under control.
feelRight: You should feel the muscles between your shoulder blades (rhomboids and mid-traps) engaging to pull the band apart, with a corresponding stretch across the front of the chest. The movement should feel smooth and controlled — think 'spread the floor with your hands' rather than just pulling the band.
feelWrong: Over-bending the elbows or arching the lower back to get more range reduces the intended stimulus on the upper back. Keep a tall posture with ribs down and chin slightly tucked — the movement is relatively small but must come from scapular retraction, not arm swing.
notes: Use a band weight that allows you to complete 15 clean reps with the last 2-3 feeling challenging. This is phenomenal prehab for overhead pressing and balances out the internal rotation demands of pushing exercises.

name: Deep Squat Holds
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, toes turned out slightly. Lower into a full-depth squat, keeping your heels on the ground and your elbows pressing against the inside of your knees to encourage an upright torso. Hold the bottom position for 30-60 seconds, breathing deeply into your belly and letting gravity pull your hips down toward your heels with each exhale.
feelRight: You should feel a deep, broadening stretch through the hips, groin, and lower back as you settle into the bottom position. Over the course of the hold, you should feel yourself gradually sinking deeper as the tissues relax and accommodate the position.
feelWrong: Rising onto your toes or letting your lower back round (butt wink) defeats the purpose of the drill — if your heels lift, widen your stance or place a small wedge under them. Letting your knees cave inward under load is a sign of insufficient hip abduction range; actively press them out against your elbows.
notes: This is the gold standard for developing squat patterning and hip mobility. If you can't hold the bottom position comfortably, use a counterweight held in front of your chest or elevate your heels slightly to work up to the full-depth position.

name: Lying Spinal Twist
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended out to the sides in a T position, palms facing up. Bend your right knee and cross it over your left side, letting gravity pull your knee toward the floor while keeping both shoulders pinned to the ground. Turn your head to look toward your right hand for a deeper rotational stretch through the spine and hold for 30-45 seconds before slowly returning to center and switching sides.
feelRight: You should feel a gentle rotational stretch through the lower back, mid-back, and glutes of the crossed leg with no sharp pain in the lower back or SI joint. The stretch should feel like a lengthening through the quadratus lumborum and spinal erectors on the stretched side.
feelWrong: Letting the opposite shoulder lift off the ground significantly reduces the spinal rotation and shifts the load into the shoulder girdle. If your shoulder pops up, place a weight or your opposite hand on it to keep it anchored, or reduce the range of the twist until both shoulders stay flat.
notes: This is an excellent cool-down or pre-sleep mobility exercise. If you feel any pinching in the lower back, try placing a small pillow under the bent knee to limit the range.

name: Scapular Wall Glides
instructions: Stand with your back against a wall, feet about 6 inches away from the wall, pressing your entire spine, sacrum, and head against the surface. Raise your arms to a goalpost position (elbows at 90 degrees, upper arms parallel to the floor) with the backs of your hands and forearms also touching the wall. Keeping all contact points with the wall, slowly glide your arms overhead as far as you can without losing contact, then return to the start position.
feelRight: You should feel the shoulder blades moving smoothly across the ribcage in a controlled, coordinated manner. The front of the chest and shoulders should feel open and stretched at the top of the movement without any pinching in the front of the shoulder joint.
feelWrong: Arching the lower back to get the arms higher is a common compensation that bypasses the intended scapular mechanics. If your arms pull away from the wall or your ribcage flares, stop at that point — the range you can maintain with full wall contact is your true mobility, not what you can force by cheating.
notes: This drill reveals exactly where your shoulder mobility ends and your lower back compensations begin. Go slowly and prioritize keeping the entire spine against the wall — the range of motion will improve over weeks of consistent practice.

name: 90/90 Hip Flow
instructions: Sit on the ground with both legs bent at 90 degrees — one leg in front of you with the knee pointing forward and the foot flexed, the other leg behind you with the knee pointing sideways and the foot resting on its side. Keeping your torso as upright as possible, gently lean forward over the front leg to deepen the external rotation stretch, then sit back and rotate your torso to face the back leg, leaning forward slightly to deepen the internal rotation stretch. Flow back and forth between these two positions in a controlled, breath-driven rhythm.
feelRight: The front leg position should produce a deep stretch through the external rotators and glutes of that hip, while the back leg position targets the deep hip capsule and groin. When both hips move freely, the transition between positions should feel fluid and progressive as the joints open up.
feelWrong: Allowing the back knee to float away from the ground or the front knee to drift inward reduces the stretch effectiveness significantly. If one side is tighter (typically the back-leg position), don't force the range — hover at the edge of discomfort and breathe into it rather than bouncing or pushing aggressively.
notes: This is one of the most effective hip mobility drills because it addresses both internal and external rotation in a single flowing movement. If the back leg position is inaccessible due to knee discomfort, place a pillow under the knee to reduce the stretch intensity.

name: Wrist Mobility Flow
instructions: Start on all fours with your palms flat on the ground, fingers spread and pointing forward. Gently rock your weight forward so your shoulders track over your wrists, feeling a stretch through the underside of the forearms and the wrist flexors. Then turn your hands so your fingers point backward (fingertips toward your knees) and rock forward again to stretch the wrist extensors. Complete the flow by rotating each hand outward and inward while keeping the palm flat to address radial and ulnar deviation, holding each position for 2-3 breaths.
feelRight: You should feel a gradual, spreading stretch through the forearm musculature and around the wrist joint without any sharp pain or pinching in the carpal bones. The sensation should open up as you breathe, with the range increasing slightly on each successive exhale.
feelWrong: If you feel sharp pain or pinching in the wrist joint itself, you're pushing too far too fast — reduce the range of motion and work at the edge of discomfort, not through pain. Collapsing into the inner edge of the hand is a common compensation; distribute your weight evenly across the entire palm and all fingers.
notes: This is invaluable for anyone doing heavy pressing, handstands, or prolonged typing. Do it as part of your warm-up before push days and as a cool-down after any session involving wrist loading.
